preamble: The shift from the voiceless velar stop [k] to a glottal stop [ʔ] is a form of debuccalization. In many English dialects, velar and dental stops in coda positions lose their oral articulation, leaving only the glottal closure.

examples.note: Proto-Malayo-Polynesian word-final *k → [ʔ] in Malay; e.g., Indonesian 'tidak' [tiˈdaʔ] (not), 'anak' [ˈanaʔ] (child).
Coda glottalization of the velar stop.
Word-final obstruents, including /k/, undergo debuccalization to /h/ or /ʔ/ at prosodic boundaries in Yucatec Maya. The stop loses its oral place of articulation, leaving only a laryngeal closure.
In branches outside K'ichean-Mamean and Qʼanjobʼalan, Proto-Mayan uvular *q merged with velar /k/, which then underwent coda weakening including glottalization in Yucatecan.

sources: Bennett, R. (2016). Mayan phonology. Language and Linguistics Compass.
